title: How to price a 'Branding Package' for a new business

interlinking_suggestions.title: How to Calculate Your Billable Rate as a Freelancer
How to Price a Graphic Design Project (Logo
How to Create a Brand Style Guide for a Client

author.name: Sofia Gonzalez
author.bio: A successful freelance creative director, Sofia provides insights for designers, writers, and artists. She covers topics like pricing creative work, protecting intellectual property, and building a powerful personal brand.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Price your services as a percentage (10-20%) of the quantifiable financial value your work will generate for the client, not based on the hours you spend.
Prevent scope creep by explicitly defining project limitations and what is not included in your proposal, using a formal 'Change Order' process for any new requests.
Secure client commitment and cash flow by mandating a 50% non-refundable deposit before any project work begins.
Include a non-negotiable contract clause that transfers intellectual property rights to the client only after the final payment has been received in full.
summary: Many creative professionals struggle with being undervalued because they bill for their time, a model that fails to capture the true business results they deliver. To solve this, the article advises adopting a three-pillar framework: **Position** your services based on client ROI, **Package** offerings to prevent scope creep, and **Protect** revenue with ironclad payment processes and contracts. Implementing this system allows you to shift the conversation from your cost to their investment, establishing you as a respected strategic partner and building a more profitable business.
ai_overview_answer:
To effectively price creative expertise, you must shift from billing for time to a value-based model that anchors your fee to the client's business results. Calculate this by quantifying the project's potential financial gain for the client and pricing your services as a percentage, typically 10-20%, of the value you help create. Establish this value by offering a paid strategy session upfront, which positions you as an expert and provides the necessary insights for your proposal. Structure your proposals into packages focused on client outcomes, such as a "Growth Engine" tier, rather than a simple list of deliverables.

Prevent scope creep by meticulously defining project boundaries, including revision limits and a formal change order process for any additional work. To protect your revenue, implement a professional payment structure starting with a mandatory 50% non-refundable deposit. For larger projects, use a milestone-based system, such as a 50/25/25 model, where payments are tied to key project phases. Reinforce these terms with an ironclad contract that specifies intellectual property rights are only transferred after final payment is complete.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

@graph.description: Learn a three-pillar framework to price branding packages based on client ROI, engineer proposals to prevent scope creep, and protect your revenue with ironclad payment processes and contracts.

step.name: POSITION: Define Your Value, Don't Just Bill Your Time
step.text: Redefine your pricing foundation by aligning your fee with the business value you create, moving the conversation from your costs to the client's return on investment.

itemListElement.text: Master Value-Based Pricing: Quantify the client's potential financial gain, estimate your direct contribution, and price your service as a 10-20% fraction of that value.
Introduce a Paid Strategy Session: Use a paid 'Brand Roadmap' or 'Strategy Blueprint' session as your gateway offer to filter serious prospects and gather deep insights for an accurate proposal.

Develop an Internal Value Proposition Matrix: Map your services (e.g., Logo System) to tangible business outcomes (e.g., Lower Customer Acquisition Cost) to lead the value conversation with confidence.

step.name: PACKAGE: Engineer Proposals That Prevent Scope Creep
step.text: Architect the engagement with a strategic proposal that manages expectations, eliminates ambiguity, and prevents scope creep to protect your time and profitability.

itemListElement.text: Structure Tiers Around Outcomes: Frame packages around strategic goals (e.g., 'The Launchpad', 'The Growth Engine') instead of generic labels to focus clients on their ambition.
Define 'What's Not Included' with Precision: Explicitly state revision cycles, specific deliverables, timelines, and a formal Change Order process to eliminate ambiguity.
Build a 'Strategic Add-Ons' Library: Offer discrete, high-value services like 'Competitor Brand Analysis' or 'Brand Naming Workshop' to customize proposals and increase project value.

step.name: PROTECT: Bulletproof Your Revenue and Get Paid Without Fail
step.text: Implement strong payment and legal structures to ensure you get paid for the value you deliver and eliminate financial anxiety.

itemListElement.text: Mandate a 50% Upfront Deposit: Make a non-refundable 50% deposit a non-negotiable requirement to confirm client seriousness, secure your schedule, and fund initial work.
Implement Milestone-Based Payments: Use a structure like 50% deposit, 25% at concept approval, and 25% before final file handover to de-risk the engagement.
Generate Globally Compliant Invoices: Use professional invoicing tools that handle legal requirements like the reverse-charge mechanism for international clients.
Include Key Protective Clauses in Your Contract: Ensure your contract includes clauses for Ownership Transfer (only after final payment), a Kill Fee for cancellations, and Limitation of Liability.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate value-based pricing?

acceptedAnswer.text: The core is shifting the conversation from your hours to their ROI. You achieve this by first collaborating with the client to quantify the project's potential financial gain (e.g., an additional $200,000 in annual revenue). Your price is then set as a small percentage of that co-created value, typically 5-15%, framing your fee as a direct investment in their success.

mainEntity.name: What must be in a branding package proposal?

acceptedAnswer.text: A professional proposal is a project agreement, not just a price list. It must include: the client's business goals, a detailed scope of work, a specific list of deliverables, and a section for exclusions defining what is *not* included. It must also feature the project timeline, a milestone-based payment schedule, and your terms of service.

mainEntity.name: How do you price a brand strategy session?

acceptedAnswer.text: Treat this as a distinct, high-value product, not a free consultation. Its purpose is to deliver immense strategic clarity upfront. A strong approach is to charge a fixed price based on 3-5 times your ideal hourly rate. This premium reflects the strategic value you provide, which is far greater than the time spent in the meeting itself.

mainEntity.name: Fixed-rate vs. value-based pricing: which is better?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are superior to hourly billing. Fixed-Rate Pricing is best for projects with a clearly defined scope, offering cost certainty. Value-Based Pricing is best for projects with a significant, measurable impact on business goals, offering the highest profit potential but requiring strong communication skills to uncover the client's potential ROI.

mainEntity.name: How do I prevent scope creep?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your best defense is a meticulously detailed proposal and contract. Explicitly define the exact deliverables, the precise number of revision rounds, and the process for handling out-of-scope work. Institute a formal 'Change Order' process in your contract, which requires any new request to be documented in a separate agreement with its own timeline and fee.

mainEntity.name: What is the best payment structure for a large project?

acceptedAnswer.text: A milestone-based payment structure is the industry standard for managing cash flow and minimizing risk. The most secure model is the 50/25/25 structure: 50% non-refundable deposit to begin work, 25% upon reaching a key interim milestone (e.g., concept approval), and the final 25% before the handover of all assets.

body.content: Command Your Value: The Three-Pillar Framework for Pricing Creative Expertise

Pricing your expertise is the most critical strategic decision you will make. Get it right, and you build a thriving, respected business. Get it wrong, and you're trapped in a cycle of being undervalued, overworked, and perpetually anxious about your bottom line.

For too long, creative professionals have been taught to bill for their time—a model that actively punishes the efficiency that comes with experience. Your value isn't measured in the hours you spend at a desk; it's measured in the tangible business results you deliver. It's time to stop being a service provider and start being a strategic partner.

This three-pillar framework is your definitive guide to transforming your business. We will show you how to **Position** your value, **Package** your offerings to eliminate risk, and **Protect** your revenue with ironclad processes. This is how you move the conversation from your cost to their investment and build a business worthy of your talent.
Pillar 1: POSITION—Define Your Value, Don't Just Bill Your Time

Before you can architect a winning proposal, you must redefine the very foundation of your pricing. This is the moment you stop trading time for money and start aligning your fee with the immense business value you create. Your goal is to move the conversation away from your costs and anchor it firmly to the client's return on investment.
Master Value-Based Pricing with This 3-Step Framework

Value pricing anchors your fee to the value you create for the client, not the time it takes you. This immediately elevates you from a hired vendor to a strategic partner. As business educators like Chris Do and Blair Enns emphasize, this requires a shift in focus from your inputs to the client's outcomes.
Here’s a simple, repeatable method to calculate your price:

items: **Quantify the Client's Potential Gain:** During initial conversations, your primary goal is to diagnose, not to sell. Ask probing questions to understand what a successful project will achieve. Are they trying to increase annual revenue by $250,000? Boost customer lifetime value by 15%? Your job is to guide them to a specific, measurable financial goal.

**Identify Your Direct Contribution:** Realistically estimate how much your work will contribute to that goal. A full brand overhaul is a significant driver of success, but it's not the only one. Be conservative yet confident. For example, you might determine your brand strategy and new identity will be responsible for 30% of that $250,000 revenue goal. The value you are helping to create is $75,000.

**Price as a Fraction of that Value:** Your final project price should be a fraction of the value you help create—typically between 10-20%. In the example above, 15% of the $75,000 value you've co-created is $11,250. This is your price. It is now completely detached from your hours and anchored firmly in their return on investment.

body.content: Introduce a Paid Strategy Session as Your Gateway Offer

The fastest way to establish your authority and gather the data needed for value-based pricing is to stop giving your thinking away for free. Offer a paid 2-4 hour intensive strategy session as the first official step.
This accomplishes several critical objectives:

items: **It filters out unserious prospects** and ensures the client is financially invested from the start.
**It positions you as an expert diagnostician,** not just a pair of hands.
**It provides the dedicated time** and access required to gather the deep insights needed for an accurate, value-based proposal.

body.content: Frame this not as a call, but as your first deliverable: a "Brand Roadmap," "Strategy Blueprint," or "Growth Plan." This de-risks the larger project for the client, proves your strategic worth, and gives you a paid mechanism to do your most important work upfront.
Develop Your Internal Value Proposition Matrix

Before you speak to a potential client, you must be fluent in the language of business outcomes. A Value Proposition Matrix is an internal tool mapping your services to the tangible results they produce. This preparation allows you to lead the value conversation with unshakable confidence.
By preparing this matrix, you arm yourself with the strategic arguments needed to justify your price. You stop selling "a logo" and start selling your contribution to their bottom line.
Pillar 2: PACKAGE—Engineer Proposals That Prevent Scope Creep

Once you've established the *why* behind your price, you must architect the *how* of the engagement. Your proposal is not a menu for the client to pick from; it is a strategic tool you engineer to manage expectations, eliminate ambiguity, and prevent scope creep. A well-crafted package structure is your primary defense mechanism for protecting your time and profitability.
Structure Tiers Around Outcomes, Not Deliverables

Abandon generic labels like "Basic, Standard, Premium." They invite commoditization and haggling. Instead, frame your packages around the client's strategic goals to shift their focus from a checklist of items to a choice about their own ambition.

rows: New businesses needing a foundational identity to attract their first customers.
Built for scaling and increasing market share.
Established businesses aiming to outpace competitors and increase revenue.
Companies seeking to solidify their position, launch new products, or command a premium price.

body.content: This reframing elevates the discussion. The client is no longer buying a logo; they are investing in a "Growth Engine." This aligns your pricing directly with their aspirations.
Define "What's Not Included" with Surgical Precision

The single most important section of your proposal is where you define the boundaries. Ambiguity is the enemy of profitability. This isn't about being difficult; it's about being a professional who provides absolute clarity for both parties.
Your "Scope & Limitations" section must explicitly state:

items: **Revision Cycles:** "This proposal includes two rounds of revisions on the selected brand concept. Additional revisions will be quoted separately."
**Specific Deliverables:** "The social media toolkit includes three static feed templates and two story templates for Instagram. It does not include content creation or account management."
**Project Timelines:** "Client feedback on all concepts is required within 48 business hours to maintain the project timeline."

**Change Order Process:** State clearly that any work requested outside the agreed scope will be quoted separately via a formal "Change Order" before work begins. This turns potential conflict into a professional transaction.

body.content: Build a "Strategic Add-Ons" Library

Resist the urge to bundle every possible service into your top-tier package. Instead, hold back discrete, high-value services that can be added to any proposal. This allows you to customize an engagement and increase its total value without bloating your core offerings.

header: Solves What Problem For The Client?
rows: "How do we effectively differentiate ourselves from our top three competitors?"
"We aren't confident our current name will resonate with our target market."
"How does our brand translate to a physical product experience?"
"We need our customer communications to look as professional as our new brand."

body.content: Having this library ready allows you to respond to specific client needs with pre-priced, high-value solutions, reinforcing your role as a proactive, strategic partner.
Pillar 3: PROTECT—Bulletproof Your Revenue and Get Paid Without Fail

A perfectly engineered proposal is useless if your payment and legal structures are weak. This final pillar ensures you get paid for the value you deliver. It’s where you eliminate financial anxiety by embedding professionalism into your payment and legal terms from the very start.
Mandate a 50% Upfront Deposit, No Exceptions

Think of the initial deposit as the formal act of a binding business commitment. Requiring a 50% non-refundable deposit is the industry standard for a reason. It accomplishes three critical objectives:

items: **It confirms client seriousness.** A client willing to invest upfront values your partnership and is committed to the project.
**It secures your schedule.** The deposit officially books the project into your calendar, reserving your valuable time and creative energy.
**It funds the initial work.** The deposit provides the necessary cash flow to begin discovery and research without forcing you to finance your client's project.

body.content: Position this as the non-negotiable threshold that every serious client must cross to engage you as a strategic partner.
Implement Milestone-Based Payments

For any large project, tying payments to progress de-risks the engagement for you. Instead of waiting for a final payment upon completion—which leaves you vulnerable—structure payments around key project phases.
A proven structure is:

items: **50% Deposit:** Paid upfront to initiate the project.
**25% Milestone Payment:** Due upon delivery and approval of the initial brand concepts.
**25% Final Payment:** Due before the handover of all final files and assets.

body.content: This tiered approach minimizes your financial exposure at every stage. You are always compensated for the work you have completed.
Generate Globally Compliant Invoices

Your professionalism is judged by your paperwork. To project authority and get paid promptly, especially by international clients, your invoices must be flawless. For example, when invoicing a business in the EU, you often need to use the **reverse-charge mechanism**, which shifts the VAT reporting responsibility to the client.
Your invoice must include:

items: Your client’s valid VAT identification number.
A clear statement such as "Reverse Charge Applies."
A VAT rate of 0%, as you are not charging them VAT directly.

body.content: Using a modern invoicing tool that automates this process is essential. It ensures you meet legal requirements, reinforcing your credibility and preventing bureaucratic friction from delaying payment.
Include Key Protective Clauses in Your Contract
Your contract is your ultimate safety net. Ensure your terms and conditions always include these three non-negotiable clauses:

items: **Ownership Transfer:** Explicitly states that you retain full intellectual property rights to all work until the final invoice is paid in full. This is your single most powerful piece of leverage to ensure final payment.
**Kill Fee:** A cancellation clause that ensures you are compensated for work performed if the client terminates the project prematurely, typically as a percentage of the total project fee.
**Limitation of Liability:** A crucial risk-management clause that caps your potential financial liability in a dispute, ensuring it cannot exceed the total fees paid for the project.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
Even with ironclad structures, specific questions always arise. This is your guide to handling the most common pricing conversations with confidence and clarity.

items:
**How do you calculate value-based pricing?**
The core is shifting the conversation from your hours to their ROI. You achieve this by first collaborating with the client to quantify the project's potential financial gain (e.g., an additional $200,000 in annual revenue). Your price is then set as a small percentage of that co-created value, typically 5-15%, framing your fee as a direct investment in their success.

**What must be in a branding package proposal?**
A professional proposal is a project agreement, not just a price list. It must include: the client's business goals, a detailed scope of work, a specific list of deliverables, and a section for exclusions defining what is *not* included. It must also feature the project timeline, a milestone-based payment schedule, and your terms of service.

**How do you price a brand strategy session?**
Treat this as a distinct, high-value product, not a free consultation. Its purpose is to deliver immense strategic clarity upfront. A strong approach is to charge a fixed price based on 3-5 times your ideal hourly rate. This premium reflects the strategic value you provide, which is far greater than the time spent in the meeting itself.

**Fixed-rate vs. value-based pricing: which is better?**
Both are superior to hourly billing, but they serve different purposes. Use this table to decide:

rows: Projects with a clearly defined and unchanging scope.
Offers cost certainty for the client and predictable cash flow for you.
Requires an extremely detailed scope of work to protect against scope creep.
Projects that create a significant, measurable impact on business goals.
Positions you as a strategic partner and offers the highest profit potential.
Requires strong communication skills to uncover the client's potential ROI.

items:
**How do I prevent scope creep?**
Your best defense is a meticulously detailed proposal and contract. Explicitly define the exact deliverables, the precise number of revision rounds, and the process for handling out-of-scope work. Institute a formal **"Change Order" process** in your contract, which requires any new request to be documented in a separate agreement with its own timeline and fee.

**What is the best payment structure for a large project?**
A milestone-based payment structure is the industry standard for managing cash flow and minimizing risk. The most secure model is the **50/25/25 structure**: 50% non-refundable deposit to begin work, 25% upon reaching a key interim milestone (e.g., concept approval), and the final 25% before the handover of all assets.

body.content: Conclusion: You Are Not a Vendor; You Are an Investment

Mastering your pricing and processes is more than a business tactic; it is a fundamental shift in your professional identity. It’s time to stop thinking like a freelancer and start operating as the CEO of a "Business-of-One."

By adopting the three-pillar framework of **Position, Package, and Protect**, you change the dynamic of every client engagement. You are no longer a commodity to be priced by the hour. You are a strategic investment with the power to generate significant returns.

The **Position** pillar is your declaration of independence from the time-for-money trap. **Package** builds on that authority by giving you absolute control over the engagement, establishing clear boundaries and managing expectations with professional precision. Finally, **Protect** makes your business resilient, ensuring your intellectual and financial efforts are never in vain through non-negotiable deposits, milestone payments, and ironclad contracts.

Together, these pillars create a powerful system that moves you from a position of uncertainty to one of control. They replace the fear of financial instability with the confidence of a seasoned professional. This is how you build a lasting, profitable, and respected creative business.
